title: Stars Behind Glass
synopsis: A small dragon gets separated from his school group during a field trip to the planetarium, finding wonder and friendship among the stars while learning that helping others matters more than staying with the crowd.

pages.text: The big doors whispered open. Ember stepped inside. The ceiling curved high above like a giant's bowl. Stars waited in the darkness. Twenty other dragons shuffled forward. Their claws clicked on smooth floors. The guide's voice echoed strange and soft. "Welcome to the planetarium," she said. Ember's amber eyes grew wide. Magic lived here. He could smell it. Sweet and electric. Like lightning caught in a jar. The baby dragon's coral-red scales shimmered. His cream belly felt warm with excitement. This place held secrets. Real ones. The kind that mattered.

illustration.description: A small friendly baby dragon with coral-red scales, a cream belly, tiny folded wings, a curly tail, and big warm amber eyes stands among a group of school dragons entering a grand planetarium lobby with tall curved ceilings and displays of planets and stars. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.

pages.text: The star show began. Lights dimmed to black. The dome came alive. Constellations bloomed overhead. Dragons gasped in wonder. Ember watched the Big Dipper pour starlight. His curly tail twitched with joy. A shooting star streaked past. Then another. The guide pointed up. "That's Orion," she whispered. "The great hunter." But Ember saw something else. A door marked "Observatory." It glowed soft blue. No one else noticed. The baby dragon took one step toward it. Then another. The star show played on. His classmates stared up. Ember stared ahead. The blue door called to him.

illustration.description: A small friendly baby dragon with coral-red scales, a cream belly, tiny folded wings, a curly tail, and big warm amber eyes watches constellations projected on a planetarium dome while other dragons look upward, and a mysterious blue-glowing door appears in the background. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: Dragons watching stars with a glowing door visible

pages.text: The door opened easily. Ember slipped through. Stairs spiraled up and up. His tiny wings fluttered as he climbed. Sunlight poured from above. Real sunlight. Not pretend stars now. At the top sat a real telescope. Big as a tree trunk. Brass and gleaming. But someone was crying. Soft sniffles echoed. Ember crept closer. A small human sat beside the telescope. She wore a name tag. "Sarah - Astronomer." Tears rolled down her cheeks. "The telescope is broken," she whispered to herself. "The children were supposed to see Saturn today." Ember's warm heart ached. This wasn't right. He had to help.

illustration.description: A small friendly baby dragon with coral-red scales, a cream belly, tiny folded wings, a curly tail, and big warm amber eyes climbs spiral stairs toward a bright observatory with a large brass telescope, where a sad young astronomer sits crying. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: Dragon climbing stairs to an observatory with a crying astronomer

pages.text: Ember stepped into the light. Sarah jumped. "Oh!" she gasped. "A dragon! But... you're so small. And kind." Her tears stopped. Ember pointed at the telescope with his tiny claw. He made soft questioning sounds. "It won't turn," Sarah explained. "The gears are stuck. I can't fix it alone. The field trip groups will be so disappointed." Ember studied the telescope. He walked around it. Once. Twice. His curious mind worked. There - a gear wheel jammed with old oil. Too thick and sticky. But dragon breath was warm. Very warm. Maybe warm enough to melt the oil. Maybe they could work together.

illustration.description: A small friendly baby dragon with coral-red scales, a cream belly, tiny folded wings, a curly tail, and big warm amber eyes examines a large brass telescope alongside a young astronomer named Sarah in a bright observatory filled with astronomical instruments. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: Dragon and astronomer examining a broken telescope together

pages.text: Ember breathed gently on the gears. Warm air, not fire. The thick oil began to soften. Sarah turned the wheel. Slowly it moved. "It's working!" she whispered. Together they adjusted the telescope. Sarah peered through. "Perfect! I can see Saturn's rings!" Footsteps echoed up the stairs. The field trip groups were coming. Ember heard his teacher calling his name. He'd been missed. But he'd helped Sarah. That felt more important than staying with the group. Sometimes helping mattered most. Sarah smiled at him. "Thank you, little dragon. You saved the day." Ember's amber eyes sparkled. Stars weren't just in the sky. They lived in moments like this too.

illustration.description: A small friendly baby dragon with coral-red scales, a cream belly, tiny folded wings, a curly tail, and big warm amber eyes breathes warm air on telescope gears while Sarah operates the controls, with the observatory bathed in golden afternoon light and other visitors climbing the stairs in the background. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: Dragon helping to fix telescope with astronomer as visitors approach

morals: Sometimes helping others is more important than following rules
Working together solves problems better than working alone

coverPrompt.description: A small friendly baby dragon with coral-red scales, a cream belly, tiny folded wings, a curly tail, and big warm amber eyes gazes up at a vast dome filled with glittering stars and swirling galaxies in a darkened planetarium. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
coverPrompt.alt: A baby dragon looking up at stars in a planetarium dome

questions.question: Where does Ember get separated from his group?
questions.explanation: Ember gets separated from his group at the planetarium during the star show.

questions.question: What is broken in the observatory?
questions.explanation: The telescope's gears are stuck with thick oil and won't turn properly.

questions.question: Who does Ember help in the story?
questions.explanation: Ember helps Sarah the astronomer fix the telescope so children can see Saturn.

questions.question: How does Ember help fix the telescope?
choices: He breathes warm air on the gears
questions.explanation: Ember breathes warm air to soften the thick oil stuck in the telescope's gears.

questions.question: What planet were the children supposed to see through the telescope?
questions.explanation: Sarah mentions that the children were supposed to see Saturn and its rings through the telescope.
